title: Simplicity and Risk
abstract: I introduce and test for preference for simplicity in choice under risk. I characterize the theory axiomatically, and derive its properties and unique predictions relative to canonical models. By designing and running theoretically motivated experiments, I document that people value simplicity in ways not fully captured by existing models that study risk premia in financial markets. Participants' risk premia increase as complexity increases, holding moments fixed; their dominance violations increase in complexity; their behavior is predicted by simplicity's characterizing axiom; and their complexity aversion is heterogeneous in cognitive ability. None of expected utility theory, cumulative prospect theory, prospect theory, rational inattention, sparsity, salience, or probability weighting that differs by number of outcomes fully capture the experimental findings. I generalize the underlying theory to additionally capture broader measures of complexity, including obfuscation, computation, and language effects.

article.introduction:
Which lottery would you find“easier”: a $1 to $10 lottery where each outcome occurs with 50% probability or an eight-outcome lottery of the form{$5.90,5%;$5.25,5%;$4.80,5%;${\lbrace \$5.90, 5\%;\$5.25, 5\%;\$4.80, 5\%;}$$4.60,5%;$3.60,5%;$3.45,55%;$2.85,5%;$${\$4.60, 5\%; \$3.60, 5\%; \$3.45, 55\%; \$2.85, 5\%;\$} $1.90,15%}${1.90, 15\%\rbrace}$? Canonical theories of choice under risk do not focus on simplicity, yet it intuitively matters. In this paper, I examine preferences for simplicity, focusing on just one dimension of simplicity: the number of components of the choice. Despite focusing on just one dimension of simplicity, I show theoretically and empirically that even this small deviation from the standard model yields substantially different predictions, which are borne out in quantitatively large magnitudes in direct experimental tests.
I show that simplicity is a unique component of choice, distinct from other drivers of individual decision making under uncertainty. I start by axiomatizing simplicity preferences and deriving their properties and predictions. My approach conceptualizes choice under risk as a trade-off between canonical considerations and lottery complexity. Simplicity preferences emerge naturally in this parsimonious setup. I then conduct several experimental tests to establish that simplicity-seeking matters above and beyond what can be captured by canonical theories, including expected utility, prospect theory (PT), cumulative prospect theory (CPT), salience, sparsity, and rational inattention. These experiments were preregistered and designed to capture simplicity-seeking behavior. I turn to these results next.
First, simplicity theory predicts that models that ignore the complexity of choices conflate risk aversion and complexity aversion. The corresponding experimental prediction is that measured risk aversion increases in number of outcomes if the analyst does not take simplicity preferences into account. I test this prediction by having experimental participants provide certainty equivalents (CEs) for 2-, 4-, 8-, and 16-outcome lotteries, which are similar in mean, variance, skewness, and range. The results of fitting a standard constant relative risk aversion (CRRA) model to lotteries with each number of outcomes is depicted in Figure1. As predicted by simplicity but not by canonical theories, I find that the estimated risk aversion inferred from these choices increases in the number of outcomes in a statistically and numerically significant way. To quantify complexity aversion, an increase in the coefficient of relative risk aversion from 0.67 to 5.93, as happens within-subject from 2 to 16 outcomes, is equivalent to a decline in CE of 54% for a lottery with a 50–50 chance of winning $1 or winning $5.
Second, simplicity theory explains dominance violations as an aversion to complexity, and predicts that these will increase as complexity increases. This prediction generalizes prior experimental results showing that, in certain contexts, individuals can prefer a dominated sure outcome to a dominating two-outcome lottery. The results show that dominance considerations are affected by complexity: participants violate dominance 1.5 times as often for 16- as for 2-outcome lotteries, and violate dominance more frequently as complexity increases.
Third, I develop predictions on the relationship between cognitive ability and complexity aversion by relating simplicity to research on working memory capacity. This working memory interpretation predicts that cognitive ability and complexity aversion may be linked, adding nuance to an earlier literature on risk aversion and cognitive ability. I test this prediction by having participants complete an incentivized standard cognitive ability module, and examining the correlation between cognitive ability and complexity aversion. The results show that cognitive ability and complexity aversion are correlated, and provide additional nuance on the relationship between cognitive ability and simplicity preferences. More precisely, low- and high-cognitive ability participants behave similarly at low levels of complexity, but deviate in behavior as complexity increases.
Fourth, I mathematically characterize simplicity agents by introducing an axiom that can be tested experimentally, termed “differences-in-differences complexity aversion.” The axiom says that, when comparing a larger and smaller support lottery, the larger support lottery's appeal should increase by more when complexity differences are removed (which can be done mathematically by carefully creating comparison lotteries that have equal complexity). I test this axiom using pairs of 2- versus 4-, 2- versus 8-, and 2- versus 16-outcome lotteries that have similar mean, variance, and skewness. TableIshows that the axiom holds either all the time or most of the time for most participants. Additional tests reject the hypothesis of expected utility-like behavior at the 0.01 level, and show that the complexity effect of moving from 4 to 16 is statistically (at thep=0.05$p=0.05$level) and numerically significant, comparable to more than doubling the average risk premium in the study. It is worth noting that the axiom is a joint test of complexity aversion and separability of risk and complexity aversion.
I also test alternative theories of choice under risk, and show that they cannot account for the results documented above. First, I test CPT (Tversky and Kahneman (1992), Barberis, Jin, and Wang (2021)) by fitting a CPT model to the data, and constructing residuals. If CPT correctly captures behavior with respect to more outcomes, there should be no relationship between these residuals and number of outcomes. However, regressing the CPT residuals on number of outcomes, clustering standard errors by individual, shows that the residuals and number of outcomes are correlated in a statistically significant way. The same holds true when including an intercept term in the regression, which captures a certainty premium over and above what is included in CPT. This indicates that neither CPT, nor CPT plus a certainty premium, is able to capture simplicity behavior. Second, I test whether probability weighting that varies by number of outcomes can explain the results by fitting a CPT model separately on each number of outcomes. The results indicate that probability weighting is similar regardless of the number of outcomes considered, indicating that differential probability weighting cannot account for the behavior predicted by simplicity.
Third, I perform the above two tests for PT (Kahneman and Tversky (1979), Grinblatt and Han (2005)). The PT residuals are statistically significantly correlated with number of outcomes (both with and without including an intercept in the residuals on number of outcomes regression), indicating that neither PT, nor PT plus a certainty premium, can explain these results. Similarly, the PT probability weighting parameter is close to identical across number of outcomes, indicating that differential probability weighting also cannot explain these results.
Fourth, I test whether the efficient coding model of cognitive noise (Frydman and Jin (2021,2023)) can capture complexity effects by testing for different probability weighting by number of outcomes, as well as via reduced-form regressions of the relative risk premia on characteristics of lotteries seen earlier in the experiment. Number of outcomes remains a statistically and numerically significant determinant of risk taking even after including these controls.
Fifth, I test whether other models of cognitive noise (such as Woodford (2012) or related models) can explain simplicity-related behavior by including an ignorance prior and correcting probabilities for this prior. For completeness, I try a variety of weights on the ignorance prior (5%, 10%, 25%, and 50% weight). After correcting for any of these weights, the pattern of increasing risk aversion in number of outcomes remains, indicating that existing models of noisy cognition cannot explain these results.
Sixth, I test whether participants are reacting to entropy costs (as opposed to costs that vary by number of outcomes) by looking at within-number-of-outcomes variation in entropy. While this variation is limited, the results indicate that participants' risk aversion does not increase in entropy in a statistically significant way, implying that the above results may be more consistent with a preference for simplicity as captured by number of possible outcomes than with the alternative notion of entropy.
Seventh, I show that salience (Klibanoff, Lamont, and Wizman (1998), Bordalo, Gennaioli, and Shleifer (2012)) cannot account for my results by deriving the salience-predicted risk premium for each lottery and showing that these do not increase in complexity with the same strength as is found in the data. I also show, however, that salience can account for some prior literature's complexity-related findings, by deriving theoretical implications of salience for event-splitting effects, and quantifying these effects numerically.
Eighth, I test whether rational inattention (Sims (2003), Veldkamp (2006), Barber and Odean (2008)) can explain complexity aversion. To do so, I consider the rows of the multiple price list (MPL) corresponding to each lottery as the choices that an individual can make for that lottery. A purely inattentive agent picks uniformly at random between these rows. A fully attentive agent picks, deterministically, the row that maximizes his utility. Therefore, if rational inattention (and in particular the cost of obtaining information) explains complexity aversion, then choice across participants should look more random as complexity increases. I test this theory by fitting a canonical rational inattention model with entropic information costs, which Matejka and McKay (2015) show is equivalent to a multinomial logit. Regardless of whether I take the value of the lottery to be expected value, or whether I take the value of the lottery to be its utility under standard parameterizations, the best-fitting cost of information under rational inattention does not increase in complexity. This indicates that canonical rational inattention models do not capture complexity aversion. Intuitively, rational inattention can explain spread, but not mean effects in choice, and complexity aversion is a mean effect.
Ninth, I examine whether sparsity can explain complexity aversion. Intuitively, sparsity is about optimal internal simplification and focusing on the most important variables. Following Gabaix (2016), I perform a Taylor expansion to determine each lottery's most important variables; standard theory says that these most important variables are the moments of the lottery. However, here I hold moments fixed even as complexity varies, indicating that sparsity is not able to fully explain these results. Intuitively, if people were optimally simplifying, they should not respond as much to external complexity manipulations as they do.
These results indicate that none of CPT, PT, varying probability weighting differing by complexity, salience, sparsity, rational inattention, cognitive noise, or entropy costs can account for a preference for simplicity as documented above.
In the financial literature, the paper contributes to a broad body of work documenting nonstandard determinants of choice under risk. This literature includes, among many other topics (see Barberis and Thaler (2003) for a review), overconfidence (Gervais and Odean (2001), Pikulina, Renneboog, and Tobler (2017)), emotion (Han (2008), Kuhnen and Knutson (2011)), and persuasive effects (Goldreich and Pomorski (2011), Schwartzstein and Sunderam (2021)), alongside some of the model types already mentioned. In this case, the nonstandard determinant I characterize, isolate, and document as quantitatively important, is simplicity. One possibly appealing feature of my findings is that the approach features minimal deviations from expected utility, yet produces strong implications for behavior, which are borne out in the data. These include implications of simplicity for measurement of risk preferences and dominance violations, which may also be useful more broadly.
Isolating and quantifying simplicity nuances prior financial work on product design (Carlin (2009), Celerier and Vallee (2017)), which finds pricing premia for complex items; this naturally raises the question whether such premia reflect consumer preference or are the result of other considerations (e.g., product differentiation concerns or inattention). Here, I separate out simplicity from the effects of these other channels. Having a formal theory makes such theoretical and empirical isolation possible, and also provides a microfoundation for other behavioral anomalies, such as those documented in this paper.
In addition to contributing to the behavioral finance literature, the paper also contributes more broadly across psychology-based economic theory and tests. For example, AppendicesA.9.-A.11.show that prior experimental work can often be explained by one of the canonical behavioral finance models I test for above. On the theoretical front, the paper contributes to a literature on choice models (Gilboa (1988), Cohen (1992), Neilson (1992), Ortoleva (2013)) by axiomatizing and introducing a representation that takes into account the complexity of a lottery. The general notion of simplicity has been appealed to (though typically not formalized and isolated) as a possible factor in other settings, such as Goldreich and Halaburda (2013) and Drexler, Fischer, and Schoar (2014). Applying simplicity preferences as developed in this paper, Goodman and Puri (2023) analyze derivative choice in a retail financial context, and Fudenberg and Puri (2022a,2022b) consider combining CPT and simplicity in a heterogeneous agent framework.1
The paper proceeds as follows. SectionIcovers the experiment; SectionIIextensions of canonical theories for complexity aversion; and SectionIIIthe theoretical model, its characterization, identification, comparative statics, and generalization. SectionIVconcludes.
article.I. Experiment:
The model used to motivate the experimental design is as-if simplicity preferences, which I introduce here to mean that an agent chooses lotteryp$p$over lotteryq$q$(writtenp≻q$p \succ q$) if and only if the expected utility ofp$p$less its complexity cost exceeds that ofq$q$, where the complexity function is positive and increasing, and is a function of the number of outcomes inp$p$, denoted#p$\# p$. The model is axiomatized in SectionIII, which also contains uniqueness and comparative statics results. However, all parts of the theory relevant for the experiment are summarized here.
Definition 1.A preference⪰$\succeq$on money lotteriesΔR$\Delta \mathbb {R}$is said to have a simplicity representation if, for any two lotteriesp,q$p, q$,p≻q⇔∑u(x)p(x)−C(#p)>∑u(x)q(x)−C(#q)$p \succ q \iff \sum u(x)p(x) - C(\# p) > \sum u(x)q(x) - C(\# q)$, whereu$u$is a continuous, strictly increasing, and unbounded below Bernoulli utility, andC:N→R$C: \mathbb {N} \rightarrow \mathbb {R}$is a weakly increasing complexity cost function.

I emphasize that this is an as-if preference, and makes no claims as to mechanism or welfare (e.g., if such behavior is intentional or a mistake). It is simply a conceptual framework for observed behavior.
This theory makes several new predictions. First, models that ignore complexity conflate risk aversion and complexity aversion. To see why, imagine eliciting a CExp$x_p$for lotteryp$p$. Under a simplicity framework, this means thatu(xp)$u(x_p)$is identical to∑u(x)p(x)−C(|support(p)|)$\sum u(x) p(x) - C(|\text{support}(p)|)$. But now ifxp$x_p$is low, one cannot distinguish whether that occurs because of high curvature of the Bernoulli utility function (what economists typically think of as risk aversion) or because of high complexity cost. Consequently, any model that ignores complexity will conflate risk aversion with complexity aversion.
Second, the theory predicts that agents may violate dominance toward simple alternatives. This occurs because, if the dominance amount is small, then the utility boost from choosing a dominating lottery may not be enough to compensate for its increased complexity. Formally, ifp$p$dominatesq$q$but is much more complex, it is possible that∑u(x)p(x)−∑u(x)q(x)$\sum u(x) p(x) - \sum u(x) q(x)$is less thanC(#p)−C(#q)$ C(\# p) - C(\# q)$, which would induce a simplicity agent to favorq$q$in their observed behavior.
Third, I characterize the theory in terms of observable behavior in SectionIII, and show that the main axiom is “differences-in-differences complexity aversion,” which states that when complexity differences are removed, a formerly more complex lottery's appeal should increase by more than that of a formerly simple lottery. One direct way to test the theory is to determine whether this characterizing axiom is satisfied empirically. SectionI.Ddiscusses the axiom in more detail, so as to discuss its content and the experimental results simultaneously.
Fourth, one possible reason lotteries with more outcomes are undesirable is cognitive constraints. If one takes a view of the theory wherein complexity cost is driven by finite cognitive resources, for example, limited working memory, then complexity aversion should decrease in cognitive ability and increase in cognitive load. This is because working memory is known to be lower in low-cognitive ability individuals (Fukuda et al. (2010), Shipstead, Harrison, and Engle (2016)), and lessens under cognitive load (Baddeley, Eysenck, and Anderson (2015)).
In the experiment, probabilities are all at least 5% and all outcomes are at least 5 cents apart. I do not make any claims on complexity regarding probabilities below this, emphasizing that the model is a conceptual framework for empirical work, and that future research may wish to explore smaller probabilities. SectionIII.Cdiscusses how one may generalize the representation to allow for continuity.
I generate lotteries with 2, 4, 8, and 16 outcomes, holding mean, variance, skewness, and range similar even while the number of outcomes varies. The intuition behind the generation procedure is, approximately, to fix the range, pick minimum and maximum lottery values corresponding to this range, and then fill in outcomes and probabilities between these values. AppendixA.1describes the exact procedure and shows that it generates lotteries whose distributions of mean, variance, skewness, and range are similar across complexity levels. The rationale behind generating lotteries this way is to help with identification of complexity effects, over and above what moments, or other theories, are able to capture.2
Participants provided CEs for these lotteries using the standard MPL procedure with enforced single switching (Holt and Laury (2002), Andersen et al. (2006)). Each MPL had six evenly spaced choices, ranging from 50 cents below the lottery's lowest payoff to the lottery's highest payoff.3Answers were incentivized in the standard way, by paying the participant their choice in one randomly chosen lottery and MPL row.
Participants were randomly assigned either to the risk-aversion module (SectionI.C), which tests the prediction that canonical models conflate risk and complexity, or to the axiom module (SectionI.D). To avoid effects arising from asymmetric presentation frequency (Frydman and Jin (2021)), in each module, participants answered an equal number of questions for each of 2-, 4-, 8-, and 16-outcome lotteries, and, as described above, these lotteries were designed to have similar payoffs, ranges, and moments across complexity levels. At the end of the survey, participants completed Raven's Advanced Progressive Matrices (APM), Set I.4To incentivize effort, participants were paid an additional $0.25 for each Raven's question answered correctly.
I preregistered the experiment, including procedure, sample size, data cleaning methods, and hypotheses on the AEA RCT Registry, Trial ID 10136. The experiment was run in September to October 2022 on Amazon Mechanical Turk (AMT).5This is an increasingly popular platform used in experimental economics (DellaVigna and Pope (2018), Snowberg and Yariv (2021)), including in behavioral research (Martinez-Marquina, Niederle, and Vespa (2019)). Several studies show that experimental results in the lab and on AMT track closely, and find that AMT can be more representative of the U.S. population than classroom settings (Berinsky, Huber, and Lenz (2012)).
I took three measures to help obtain high-quality data. First, I followed best-practice recommendations for AMT (Brick et al. (2021), Moss et al. (2021)), restricting the survey to workers based in the United States, with at least 95% approval rating on prior tasks and at least 100 prior completed tasks, and who did not fail any of CloudResearch's screening questions. Second, I included three comprehension checks, screening out participants who fail these. Third, I paid an unusually high amount by AMT standards: the average total payment was $9.33 for an average survey time of 23 minutes, compared to a typical 20-minute wage of $3 to $4 (Moss et al. (2020)). Using the preregistered data cleaning criteria, the final sample size was 582. This final sample reflected a diversity of gender (48% female), age (54% under 40), education (60% college educated), and income (61% earning under $75k per year).
In the risk-aversion module, participants provide CEs for thirty-two 2-, 4-, 8-, and 16-outcome lotteries (eight for each number of outcomes). I fit an expected utility model to just the two-outcome lotteries, then just to the four-outcome lotteries, and so on, using the standard CRRA specificationU(p)=∑p(x)x1−α1−α$U(p) = \sum p(x) \frac{x^{1-\alpha }}{1-\alpha }$,α≥0$\alpha \ge 0$, with largerα$\alpha$denoting greater risk aversion. I follow the procedure of Bruhin, Epper, and Fehr-Duda (2010) in fitting this model, which allows for individual-specific errors (details in AppendixA.3).6Using CRRA risk parameters is standard procedure in experimental and empirical literatures (see, e.g.,Neilson and Stowe (2002), Chetty (2006), Fehr-Duda and Epper (2012), Barseghyan et al. (2018)) and therefore this is a relevant comparison for the literature.
The numerical estimates and corresponding 95% confidence intervals of the CRRA parameter, for each number of outcomes are 0.67 [0.38, 1.0], 1.38 [1.05, 1.71], 3.21 [2.68, 3.84], and 5.93 [5.06, 7.07] for 2, 4, 8, and 16 outcomes, respectively, and are depicted in Figure1. As predicted by simplicity theory, measured “risk aversion” increases in number of outcomes in a numerically and statistically significant way because the model does not explicitly account for complexity, and thereby conflates risk aversion and complexity aversion.7To contextualize these jumps, an increase in the coefficient of relative risk aversion from 0.67 to 1.38, as happens within-subject from 2 to 16 outcomes, is equivalent to a decline in CE of 20% for a lottery with a 50–50 chance of winning $1 or winning $5. Moving from four outcomes to eight outcomes, participants' increase in measured risk aversion (from 1.38 to 3.21) is equivalent to a further decrease in the CE by 32% for a lottery with a 50–50 chance of winning $1 or winning $5, and from 8 to 16 outcomes, a further decrease of 15%. An increase in the coefficient of relative risk aversion from 0.67 to 5.93, as happens within-subject from 2 to 16 outcomes, is equivalent to a decline in CE of 54% for a lottery with a 50–50 chance of winning $1 or winning $5.8
The risk-aversion estimate for two outcomes matches values found in earlier experimental literature using two-outcome lotteries: atα=0.67$\alpha = 0.67$, it is close to, for example, Camerer and Ho (1994), who findα=0.68$\alpha = 0.68$, and statistically indistinguishable from Wu and Gonzalez (1996), Bruhin, Epper, and Fehr-Duda (2010), and Fehr-Duda and Epper (2012).9For four outcomes only and eight outcomes only, theα$\alpha$estimate is in the 1 to 4 range, similar to estimates in the public economics (Chetty (2006), Barseghyan et al. (2018)) and macrofinance (Kaplan and Violante (2014)) literatures. At 16 outcomes, the risk-aversion parameter is nearly 6, much higher than that found in typical empirical or experimental studies (Neilson and Stowe (2002), Chetty (2006), Harrison and Rutstrom (2009)). Note that empirical studies often include wealth when calculating risk aversion, while experimental studies, including this one, tend to use narrow framing. For this reason, empirical and experimental risk-aversion estimates are not directly comparable; above two outcomes, the comparisons serve only to contextualize the numbers.
That aversion to lotteries increases in complexity is inherent to the data, and not an artifact of the structural model. FigureA.2shows that, in the raw data, the relative risk premium (RRP) increases in number of outcomes in a numerically and statistically significant way, nearly doubling from two to eight outcomes, and increasing again at 16.Internet Appendix IA.B.1provides further robustness checks showing that many participants have a higher willingness to pay for simple lotteries, even when they are objectively worse than more complex ones.10
The main axiom characterizing the simplicity representation (Theorem1) is differences-in-differences complexity aversion. The axiom says that, when comparing a larger and smaller support lottery, the larger support lottery's appeal should increase by more when complexity differences are removed.

Participants perform the above procedure for three pairs of 2- versus 4-outcome lotteries, three pairs of 2- versus 8-outcome lotteries, and three pairs of 2- versus 16-outcome lotteries, again generated so that moments and ranges are not statistically distinguishable across complexity levels (AppendixA.1). Eighty-four percent to ninety percent of participants satisfy the axiom for at least 2/3 pairs for each 2 versusn$n$comparison,n∈{4,8,16}$n \in \lbrace 4, 8, 16\rbrace$. Among these, 57% to 59% strictly adhere to the axiom (c>c′$c > c^{\prime }$) for at least one of their three choices at each 2 versusn$n$comparison. Complexity aversion is monotonic, with the differencec−c′$c - c^{\prime }$at an average of $0.005 for 2 versus 4 outcomes, $0.012 for 2 versus 8 outcomes, and $0.146 for 2 versus 16 outcomes. The difference between 2 versus 4 and 2 versus 8 is statistically significant at thep<0.01$p < 0.01$level, as is 2 versus 4 against 2 versus 16. Observe that this is an especially demanding test because, by Theorem1, behaving according to the characterizing axioms means that the agent is not subject to any other behavioral biases. In practice, we know that other behavioral biases exist, and follow-up work (Fudenberg and Puri (2022a)) explores combining simplicity with some of these.
A large empirical (Anagol, Cole, and Sarkar (2017), Bhargava, Loewenstein, and Sydnor (2017), Egan (2019)) literature documents that people violate dominance in certain settings. Simplicity provides a rationale for such violations—people value ease of processing—and generalizes prior results in the experimental literature.
Within experiments, the uncertainty effect documents that, between-subjects, a dominated sure outcome can be chosen more frequently than a dominating two-outcome lottery (see, e.g., Gneezy, List, and Wu (2006)). Simplicity generalizes this effect by predicting that, even within-subject, participants may choose dominated sure outcomes more frequently as complexity increases. Figure2plots the average frequency with which reported CEs are dominated by number of outcomes; it is possible to measure this in my experimental design because I deliberately had the MPL start at 50 cents below the worst outcome in the lottery, rather than the more usual practice of not allowing participants to report dominated CEs (Holt and Laury (2002), Bruhin, Epper, and Fehr-Duda (2010)). At two outcomes, participants choose dominated CEs 3.9% of the time on average, with that frequency increasing to 4.4% for four outcomes, 5.2% for eight outcomes, and 6.4% for 16 outcomes. To give a concrete example, no one chose a dominated CE for the two-outcome lottery {$8.40, 0.85; $5.40, 0.15}, but 8% of participants reported a willingness to pay of strictly less than $6.95 for the eight-outcome lottery {$8.95, 0.05; $8.70, 0.4; $7.60, 0.2; $7.40, 0.05; $7.35, 0.05; $7.25, 0.05; $7.10, 0.05; $6.95, 0.15}. Clustering standard errors by individual, the increase in dominance violations from 2 to 16 outcomes is marginally statistically significant at thep=0.08$p = 0.08$level.11
An alternative way to analyze dominance violations is to look at lotteries that dominate each other, and check whether participants provide higher CEs for the dominating lottery. However, because in the experimental design I deliberately hold moments fixed as complexity varies, there are few dominance relations of this form in my lottery set (AppendixA.5describes and analyzes these).
While this paper is not primarily about mechanisms—focusing instead on showing that simplicity preferences exist and are not well captured by existing theories—in this section, I provide evidence that measured cognitive ability correlates with complexity aversion, which may provide a starting point for future work. I also discuss one possible underlying mechanism (but do not claim that this is unique; there may be other mechanisms underlying complexity aversion).
In cognitive psychology, working memory refers to the system humans use to temporarily hold and process information (Baddeley, Eysenck, and Anderson (2015)). Capacity is measured in chunks, defined as “meaningful items” (Cowan (2010)). Early research put capacity at seven chunks (Miller (1956)) while contemporary research puts capacity nearer to four (Gilchrist, Cowan, and Naveh-Benjamin (2008), Cowan (2010), Baddeley, Eysenck, and Anderson (2015)). If a task requires storage of information above capacity, additional cognitive resources are recruited at weakly positive cognitive cost (Jaeggi et al. (2007)). A working memory interpretation of simplicity posits that an aversion to more outcomes may result from the additional mental space they take. This interpretation of complexity cost may help explain a surprising but repeated experimental finding: that people with higher cognitive ability are less risk averse (Boyle et al. (2011), Dohmen et al. (2018)). The working memory interpretation of simplicity theory offers a possible explanation: working memory capacity tends to be higher in individuals with greater cognitive ability (Fukuda et al. (2010), Shipstead, Harrison, and Engle (2016)), and therefore higher cognitive ability individuals may be less complexity averse. This lower complexity aversion would manifest as lower “risk aversion” when CE measures are used to elicit risk attitudes, as they are in the studies cited.
The experiment tests whether cognitive ability and complexity aversion are correlated. Figure3splits participants in the risk-aversion module by cognitive ability—above or below median, respectively12—and plots measured risk aversion by number of outcomes by performing the exercise of SectionI.Cfor each cognitive group. The result at two outcomes replicates that of the above literature, for example, low-cognitive ability individuals have higher measured “risk aversion.” However, as complexity increases to 8 and 16 outcomes, the discrepancy in CRRA-measured risk aversion further diverges, indicating that complexity aversion and measured cognitive ability are weakly negatively correlated. The difference in measured risk aversion between the low- and high-cognitive ability groups is 0.86 at two outcomes (marginally statistically significant at thep<0.1$p < 0.1$level), rising to 2.02 at eight outcomes (statistically significant at thep<0.05$p < 0.05$level), and 3.83 at 16 outcomes (statistically significant at thep<0.05$p < 0.05$level). The difference at four outcomes is not statistically distinguishable from zero. Low-cognitive ability individuals treat two and four outcomes similarly from a complexity perspective, but are highly complexity averse at 8 and 16 outcomes; this pattern is not observed in high-cognitive ability individuals.
Looking only at Figure1, which shows that measured risk aversion increases in complexity, it is not clear whether one should model risk and complexity as having interaction effects or as separable components of observed preference. If complexity and risk are separable, as they are in the conceptual framework of Definition1, then, after controlling for complexity, the risk-aversion estimate should be identical no matter which level of complexity was controlled for.
I perform this exercise separately on eight outcomes and on 16 outcomes, using maximum likelihood to estimate bothC(2)$C(2)$andα$\alpha$. On eight outcomes,C(2)$C(2)$andα$\alpha$are estimated at 0.07 and 0.11, with standard error 0.02 and 0.07 respectively.13On 16 outcomes, the corresponding estimates forC(2)$C(2)$andα$\alpha$are 0.05 and 0.21, with standard error 0.03 and 0.17. Theseα$\alpha$estimates are not statistically distinguishable and are numerically similar; at a difference of 0.1, the estimates are much closer than the 2.7 difference in measured risk aversion in Figure1, when complexity costs were not explicitly accounted for. This provides some evidence that risk and complexity aversion are largely separate components of choice.
article.II. Canonical Theories and Complexity Av:
I consider the implications of canonical theories for complexity aversion and show that neither they nor natural extensions can explain the results found here.14These theories have a literature behind them, often with convincing evidence in other contexts. I do not contest any of this; this section only answers the very narrow question of whether canonical theories can capture complexity aversion as documented here.
Expected utility would predict identical risk aversion regardless of the number of outcomes, which is rejected by the statistically significant increase in risk-aversion parameters across outcomes. This is shown parametrically under CRRA models in Figure1, and nonparametrically in FigureA.2.
A large asset pricing literature (Kraus and Litzenberger (1976), Harvey and Siddique (2000), Boyer, Mitton, and Vorkink (2010)) documents a significant skewness premium in empirical capital asset pricing models. This means that the market in aggregate (e.g., as conceptualized by a representative agent) is willing to pay more for positively skewed bets. This market-wide preference for skewness may, but need not, arise from probability weighting (Barberis and Huang (2008)). As mentioned in the Introduction, prior results on complexity can often plausibly be captured by a skewness preference because in prior work, skewness and complexity covary. However, in this experiment, I am careful to hold skewness fixed as I vary complexity. The complexity results shown here are therefore net of any skewness preference.
The rank dependence feature of CPT implies a (not necessarily monotonic) sensitivity to the number of outcomes (Bernheim and Sprenger (2020)). Following Fudenberg and Puri (2022a), to test whether participants display a preference for simplicity over and above what CPT can capture, I fit a CPT model with the classic Tversky and Kahneman (1992) formulation; this isUCPT(p)=∑u(xi)π∑k=1ipk−π∑k=1i−1pk$U_{CPT}(p) = \sum u(x_i) \left[ \pi \left(\sum _{k=1}^i p_k \right) - \pi \left(\sum _{k=1}^{i-1} p_k \right) \right]$(where outcomes are in decreasing order, withu(x)=xα,α∈[0,1]$u(x) = x^\alpha, \alpha \in [0,1]$andπ(p)=pξpξ+(1−p)ξ1ξ$\pi (p) = \frac{p^\xi }{\left(p^\xi + (1-p)^\xi \right)^\frac{1}{\xi }}$,ξ∈[0,1]$\xi \in [0,1]$)15to the data from the risk-aversion module and calculate the CPT residual as the predicted CPT value less the true reported CE. If the true data-generating process is CPT, then there should be no correlation between residuals and number of outcomes. If the true data-generating process is CPT plus a certainty premium, then, after allowing for an intercept term, there should be no correlation between residuals and number of outcomes. However, regressing these residuals on the number of outcomes yields a statistically significant slope of 0.03 withp$p$-value<$<$0.01.16Similarly, regressing on number of outcomes, allowing for an intercept, yields a coefficient on number of outcomes of 0.014 withp$p$-value<$<$0.01. These results indicate that neither CPT nor CPT plus a certainty premium correctly captures variation with respect to complexity.
The above test imposed the same probability weighting parameter across all outcomes. An alternative theory that extends CPT is that the probability weighting parameter varies by complexity. To test this, I estimate the CPT model on each number of outcomes separately. While the standard errors are large (possibly due to limited within-outcome variation by which to identify two parameters (risk aversion and probability weighting)), AppendixA.10plots the results of this test and shows that the probability weighting parameter is approximately the same (both pointwise and after taking standard errors into account) for each number of outcomes. These results indicate that differential probability weighting by number of outcomes cannot explain complexity aversion as documented here.
As in the test of CPT, I test for PT's ability to explain simplicity-related behavior in two ways.17First, I fit a PT model to the entire data set, construct residuals, and test whether these residuals are statistically significantly correlated with the number of outcomes. Second, I test whether complexity-varying probability weighting can explain the risk-aversion results. The PT residuals test yields a slope of 0.05 withp$p$-value<$<$0.01 when regressing residuals on number of outcomes. The slope andp$p$-value remain similar (0.03 and<$<$0.01, respectively) when including an intercept term. This indicates that neither PT, nor PT plus a certainty premium, can capture preference for simplicity as modeled here.
The second test finds that PT probability weighting parameter is statistically indistinguishable for each number of outcomes (AppendixA.10). This indicates that differential PT probability weighting by complexity also cannot account for a preference for simplicity.
Bordalo, Gennaioli, and Shleifer (2012) introduce a theory of salience and show that it can capture a number of empirical regularities in the experimental literature. As I prove in AppendixA.11, for certain types of event splitting, salience also predicts complexity aversion. To check whether salience captures the complexity aversion results in this experiment, I use a residuals test, calculating salience predictions less actual CEs, and regressing these residuals on number of outcomes. The regression yields a positive and statistically significant slope, indicating that the data show complexity aversion beyond what canonical salience theory is able to capture. In addition, AppendixA.11proves that salience cannot capture dominance violations of the form documented in SectionI.Ebecause it does not allow for such dominance violations.
Several papers study rational inattention effects on choice (Sims (2003), Veldkamp (2006), Barber and Odean (2008), among others). Matejka and McKay (2015) show that, under the canonical rational inattention model with cost of informationλ$\lambda$, choice between actionsai$a_i$takes the form of a multinomial logit. To map the canonical rational inattention model to the experiment, take states of nature to be the agent's preferences, which may take introspection or effort to determine. Take actions to be rows of the MPL (i.e., the participant must determine at which row to switch from the safe choice to the lottery). Let the prior be uniform over all rows.18Since the price lists have the same increments and range across number of outcomes, the actions that can be taken are similar regardless of complexity, and the only part of the model that remains that can vary with complexity isλ$\lambda$, the cost of information acquisition. In particular, if canonical rational inattention explains complexity aversion, then, in a maximum likelihood estimation, the cost of acquiring information,λ$\lambda$, should increase with complexity. I perform this exercise in AppendixA.12for several candidates for the participant's true preferencesu$u$, including maximizing bonuses, risk-averse CRRAu$u$, risk-averse CPTu$u$, and risk-averse PTu$u$, all with parameters taken from the literature. Regardless of which true preference is specified, the best-fitting cost of information acquisitionλ$\lambda$does not increase in complexity. Intuitively, in the experiment, measured risk aversion increases in complexity, while canonical rational inattention captures randomness relative to some canonical model, and it is difficult for a model about spreads to explain mean effects.
Gabaix (2014) proposes that attention-constrained agents pay attention only to the most important variables in making their decisions. There is some degree of flexibility in what most important means in the lottery context, but one reasonable definition, following Gabaix (2016), is that the agent focuses on those variables that a Taylor approximation identifies as most important. For a canonical agent, these are the moments of the lottery. However, here I hold moments of lotteries fixed even as complexity varies, meaning that the results documented here are net of sparsity considerations. More generally, sparsity is about internal simplification; if agents were successfully simplifying lotteries internally, then they should not be exhibiting the large increases in risk aversion observed in the experiment.
Several papers explore how larger menus affect choice (Iyengar and Kamenica (2010), Goldreich and Halaburda (2013)). Extending this argument, the closest object to a menu in the experiment is the MPL used to elicit willingness to pay. However, this “menu” stays fixed even as lottery complexity varies, showing that even after controlling for menu considerations, the composition of the comparison object—here, the lottery—affects choice.
Frydman and Jin (2023) posit that the perception of probabilities depends on a prior, which, because of an information processing constraint, is only partially updated when the agent sees the true probability. Consequently, the perceived probability, and more generally properties of the lottery, can be dependent on the shape of the prior, and that prior can change with experience. In AppendixA.13, I devise and conduct five empirical tests for this theory's ability to capture complexity aversion. In addition to taking the theory literally, I also test more generally for within-experiment experience effects. The results indicate that, while some efficient coding may be occurring, complexity remains an important component of behavior even after coding and past seen lotteries are taken into account.
Prior theories of cognitive noise postulated that agents have a prior of approximately uniform probabilities for all outcomes, which may not be fully updated with the true probability values of the lottery. Woodford (2012) and Enke and Graeber (2023), among others, show that, under certain distributions for priors and signals, the agent's perceived probability is a linear combination of the true probability and the prior. Under this hypothesis, after correcting for the ignorance prior, measured risk aversion should not increase in number of outcomes. I rerun the analysis in SectionI.C, replacing all probabilitiesp$p$withp′$p^{\prime }$, wherep′=κp+(1−κ)1#Outcomes$p^{\prime } = \kappa p + (1-\kappa) \frac{1}{\#\text{ Outcomes}}$, so that each cognitively uncertain probability hasκ$\kappa$weight on the true value and1−κ$1 - \kappa$weight on uniformity. FigureA.6, Panel A, performs this correction for a wide variety of weightsκ$\kappa$(5%, 10%, 25%, and 50%), and shows that, even after these corrections, the pattern of increasing risk aversion by number of outcomes remains, indicating that cognitive noise as postulated this way cannot account for these results.19
Another possible way to model simplicity is with entropy-based costs (Manonen (2023)). Under this theory, higher entropy should increase observed risk aversion. Within each number of outcomes, there is some variation in entropy, so one partial test of this theory is to analyze the correlation between entropy and relative risk premia within a particular number of outcomes. This correlation being positive would be evidence in favor of an entropy cost theory. However, this coefficient, while positive at eight outcomes, is negative at 2, 4, and at 16 outcomes, the opposite of what entropy costs would predict. As the variation in entropy within a given number of outcomes is limited in these data, this test is far from conclusive, but it does suggest that participants in this experiment are reacting to complexity as captured by number of outcomes, rather than to entropy.
article.III. Theory:
The experiments above showed that the simplicity representation (Definition1) and its characterizing axiom (Section I.D) predict behavior which canonical theories do not fully capture (SectionII). This section completes the characterization theorem, and generalizes the theory to allow for broader notions of complexity.

To generalize the model, assume that lotteries are partitioned intocognitive tiersT⊆R$\mathbb {T} \subseteq \mathbb {R}$. I do not specify what a cognitive tier entails, wherein arises the flexibility of the model. For example, Tier 1 could be English-language lotteries and Tier 2 lotteries described in Latin. Tier 1 could be lotteries whose outcomes are multiples of five and higher tiers could include outcomes with many decimal places. Or, Tier 1 could be direct language and higher tiers obfuscating language. The partition is flexible.
It is easy to see that the simplicity representation generalizes to tiers, for example, by changing everywhere support size #p$p$to Tier(p$p$). The characterizing axiom remains the same, with tier replacing cardinality. For axiomatization only (the representation and its implications can be used for any notion of complexity), the only imposition placed is that lotteries with the same support be in the same tier. The axioms therefore allow for arbitrary support- or description-based notions of complexity, but not probability-based notions.21Formally, Definition3introduces the general simplicity representation, which is characterized and extended to include description-based notions of complexity in AppendixB.1.22
Definition 3.A preference⪰$\succeq$on money lotteriesΔR$\Delta \mathbb {R}$is said to have ageneral simplicity representationif, for any lotteriesp,q$p, q$,p≻q⇔∑u(x)p(x)−C(Tier(p))>∑u(x)q(x)−C(Tier(q))$p \succ q \iff \sum u(x)p(x) - C({Tier(p)}) > \sum u(x)q(x) - C({Tier(q)})$, whereu$u$is continuous, strictly increasing, and unbounded below, andC:N→R$C: \mathbb {N} \rightarrow \mathbb {R}$is a weakly increasing complexity cost function.

To demonstrate how this generalization can extend beyond the risk space, I provide two examples.
Example 1. (Computational Complexity)Suppose that the setting is deterministic: the agent receives $x,x∈R$x \in \mathbb {R}$, but that amount of money can be described either directly (e.g. $29) or as a mathematical expression withn$n$operations (e.g. $14 + 11 + 4). Let the prize space be∪n∈NR×{+,−,∗,÷}N$\cup _{n \in \mathbb {N}} \left(\mathbb {R} \times \lbrace +,-,*,\div \rbrace \right)^{\mathbb {N}}$with generic elemente$e$, that is, mathematical expressions; letx(e)$x(e)$denote the value that the expression evaluates to, andn(e)$n(e)$the number of mathematical operations in the expression. A generalized simplicity representation is able to capture an aversion to computation asU(expression e)=u(x(e))−C(n(e))$U(\text{expression e}) = u(x(e)) - C(n(e))$.
Though deterministic, the analog to the risk-based representation is clear: there is an objective component, and a penalization for the “number of things to think about,” in this case, the number of mathematical terms. As a result, analogs of the risk-based theoretical properties derived earlier would also hold for computational complexity. For example, an agent may be less willing to pay for a more computationally intensive asset even if it evaluates to the same amount of money as an asset whose payoffs are more transparently described.
The stochastic extension of simplicity preferences (AppendixB.2) can further capture the idea that noise increases as computational requirements increase.


Example 2. (Language Effects)Another colloquial measure referred to as complexity is the use of technical terminology (see, e.g., plainlanguage.gov (2023)). To capture this, let there be two tiers; tier 0 refers to a direct description, and tier 1 to a description involving technical terminology (one could also haven$n$tiers, with the tier level corresponding to the number of technical terms used). Then, lettingL=R×D$L = \mathbb {R} \times D$denote the prize space, wherex∈R$x \in \mathbb {R}$is the payoff andD$D$the description tier, the generalized simplicity representation captures language effects asU(l)=u(x(l))−C(d(l))$U(l) = u(x(l)) - C(d(l))$, wherex(l)$x(l)$is the payoff corresponding to linguistic descriptionl$l$andd(l)$d(l)$is its level of technicality. For example, this representation predicts that individuals may be willing to pay more for a health care plan described as “you pay 100% of medical expenses up to the first $100, after which you pay 20%” than one that “has a deductible of $100 and coinsurance rate of 20%,” though these contain precisely the same information.

Future work could empirically test these or other notions of complexity using the theory developed here to obtain predictions and aid in identification.
It is possible to generalize the representation further still. For example, there may be complexity effects within as well as across tiers. AppendixB.3introduces a cognitive tiers representation of simplicity, which says that the linear core of the preference becomes smaller as complexity increases. By taking the space of cognitive tiers to be the continuum, one can obtain representations where complexity aversion is continuous in the cognitive tier of the lottery (e.g., as in Example 2 of AppendixB.3). Consequently, more complex options are harder to evaluate, and this more general framework allows for dominance violations within a complexity tier (e.g., 16 vs. 16 outcomes, as discussed in AppendixB.5).23
Similarly, AppendixB.2extends simplicity to stochastic representations which predict that noise also increases in complexity.
article.IV. Conclusion:
This paper documents a preference for simplicity in choice under risk, and shows that observed behavior is not fully captured by canonical behavioral finance models. Observed risk aversion increases in complexity, as do the frequency of dominance violations. Measured cognitive ability and complexity aversion are correlated at higher complexity levels. None of: expected utility theory, CPT, PT, skewness preferences, salience, sparsity, rational inattention, cognitive noise, or complexity-varying probability weighting, fully capture simplicity-seeking behavior. I introduce models of as-if simplicity preferences in choice under risk, characterize them, and empirically test the characterizing axiom in the special case where complexity is taken to be number of outcomes (though the theory itself is more general). I also generalize and extend the theory, for example, to apply to nonrisk settings.
There are a wide variety of ways to build on this work to better understand individual behavior. For example, one could perform similar tests for alternate notions of complexity, such as the language effects captured by the general model, which may be relevant for company filings. Future work could study cognitive mechanisms underlying simplicity preferences, and the extent to which they are inherent. The idea of simplicity as a counting or disaggregation measure (captured in the experiment as number of outcomes) may also generalize well. I conclude by speculating on possible directions for future work across financial subfields below.
Portfolio Choice. There are in some cases products which literally have fewer possible payoffs than other products. For example, Goodman and Puri (2023) analyze binary options—two-outcome lotteries—and show that retail markets with limited entry empirically feature a price premium for these, relative to what standard theories would predict. More generally, applying complexity either as “number of things to think about” or as “computational difficulty” (as captured by the general model) may be promising for household finance, where this measure may interact with institutional details. For example, the number of things to think about in cases where one has an investment advisor or automation is different from the number of active choices or things to think about in settings without these. In addition, the theory is able to capture not only pricing differences (as complexity aversion) but also errors in optimization (through the stochastic model of AppendixB.2). Additional work both on the predictions of the theory developed here (e.g., as disaggregation, computational difficulty, or ease-of-processing effects), alternative methods of thinking about simplicity, and understanding how to combine simplicity with institutional detail in other settings would be useful.
Incentive Schemes. A counting measure naturally applies to incentive schemes, for example, as the number of piecewise linear components in a compensation function. Piecewise linear functions are used, for example, in the employer match in 401(k) savings in household finance, as well as in executive compensation schemes in corporate finance. Future work could examine the impact of more components (keeping average or total compensation fixed) on behavioral patterns of interest.
The Design and Provision of Information. A large literature in finance is concerned with how agents update their beliefs in the face of information. This includes, but is not limited to, provision of information on climate change, on investment instruments such as student or payday loans, company filings, or trading performance. One could use the theory developed in this paper to identify complexity in a structured way. For example, consider providing the same information, but in one setting that information has fewer parts than in another setting (e.g. “$29” vs. “$52+4$\$5^2 + 4$”; or two documents providing the same information but one more accessible than the other). As discussed in SectionIII.C, such disaggregation, computation, and accessibility measures are natural extensions of the disaggregation measure studied in the experiment, which are captured by the general theory and may be of interest in the study of reaction to information.
Market Intermediation. One service that brokers or investment advisors provide is simplification—or disaggregation—of decisions. For example, rather than describing all the characteristics of a financial product and leaving it to the individual to perform the deduction, an investment advisor can perform calculations on behalf of the individual or household. In both cases, the financial product is the same, so one can argue that this relates to simplicity as conceptualized here. Reducing such disaggregation is, in theory, a value-added service whose price can be measured.
This is only a partial list of possible topics for future study. Simplicity has the potential to enrich understanding across a variety of financial subfields, and is a rich avenue for future research.

article.A.1. Lottery Generation Procedure:
The risk-aversion module had 32 lotteries, with 8 for each number of outcomes inN={2,4,8,16}$N = \lbrace 2,4,8,16\rbrace$. The axiom module had 12 lotteries, with 3 for each number of outcomes inN$N$. To generate additional diversity in the lotteries, in the certainty equivalents (CEs) module the payoffs ranged from 1 to 15, and in the axiom module, the payoffs ranged from 1 to 10.
In the second step, I manually adjusted the lotteries to help ensure that the moment distributions and ranges matched across complexity levels, and that each complexity level had (to the extent possible) a diversity of probabilities.
The resulting lotteries are in TableIA.II, of which the first 32 were used in the risk-aversion module, and the last 12 in the axiom module.
FigureA.1plots the moments of the lotteries by complexity level.
In addition to generating lotteries in a manner that makes moments similar across complexity levels,24the method of analysis also strives to take account of moments by using, in SectionI.C, a structural CRRA approach. This approach accounts for moments under the null hypothesis that the agent is an expected utility maximizer (other behavioral null hypotheses are considered in SectionII).Internet Appendix IA.B.1provides additional robustness tests in which more complex lotteries are objectively better, and nevertheless many participants display higher willingness to pay for more simple, but objectively worse, lotteries.
article.A.2. Nonparametric Analysis: I calculate the relative risk premium (RRP),(EV−CE)/EV$(EV - CE)/EV$for each individual and lottery. FigureA.2plots the results of regressing RRP on number of outcomes, controlling for variance and skewness and clustering standard errors by individual. RRPs are statistically significantly higher for eight than for two outcomes, and for 16 than for two outcomes. At 0.03, the RRP for two outcomes is in the range of that found by Fehr-Duda and Epper (2012), a review article on experiments studying prospect theory (PT) with two-outcome lotteries. The pattern of increasing RRP by number of outcomes persists (though loses statistical significance) when not controlling for moments. The CRRA model used in the main text implicitly controls for moments, as revealed by a standard Taylor expansion for expected utility.
article.A.3. Econometric Model: This model is estimated using maximum likelihood via the expectation-maximization algorithm (Dempster, Laird, and Rubin (1977)). Point estimates and 95% confidence intervals are obtained using the bootstrap method (Efron and Tibshirani (1993), DiCiccio and Efron (1996)) with 1,000 iterations. For numerical stability, in estimation, I setα$\alpha$’s maximum value to 10, which is well above any experimental or empirical estimate (Chetty (2006), Fehr-Duda and Epper (2012), Kaplan and Violante (2014)); unrestricted maximumα$\alpha$values yield qualitatively similar, though less precise results.25Intuitively, in this parametric model, the mean risk-aversion estimate is pooled across individuals, but each individual is allowed their own error term.
article.A.4. Axiom Module Robustness Checks:
I test the alternative hypotheses (i) that people were behaving as per expected utility, (ii) that people were clicking randomly, and (iii) that measurement error drives these results. For (i), expected utility would predictc=c′$c = c^{\prime }$for all pairs and people. However, the percentage of people for whomc=c′$c = c^{\prime }$for all pairs is 26% for 2 versus 4 outcomes, 25% for 2 versus 8 outcomes, and 26% for 2 versus 16 outcomes. These are all statistically significantly different from 100% at thep<0.01$p < 0.01$level. One can also test the hypothesis of expected utility with mean-zero additive noise, in which case an individual should act per EU for a majority of pairs, though may not act per EU for all pairs. Fifty-eight percent of people satisfyc1=c2$c_1 = c_2$for a majority of 2 versus 16 outcome pairs; 59% for 2 versus 8 outcomes; and 58% for 2 versus 4 outcomes. These are again all statistically significantly different from 100% withp$p$-values below 0.01.
For (ii), if participants were clicking randomly, the percentage of people satisfying the axiom for the majority of pairs would be 50%. However, this 50% figure is rejected at thep<0.01$p<0.01$level for each of 2 versus 4 outcomes, 2 versus 8 outcomes, and 2 versus 16 outcomes. For (iii), for measurement error to drive the results, one would need differential error between the axiom-predicted better and worse lotteries. However, I hold MPL increments fixed within pair comparisons: that is, the measurement error is identical for the better and worse lotteries, and also for the more and less complex lotteries that help build these.
article.A.5. Between-Lottery Relationships:
The main text considered dominance violations where the participant's stated CE was strictly below the worst outcome in the lottery. An alternate form of dominance violation is where one nondegenerate lottery dominates another nondegenerate lottery, but the participant provides a higher CE for the former. There are several challenges analyzing the latter form of dominance violation in this data set. First, because moments are held similar as complexity changes, few such dominance relations exist between lotteries. Second, between-lottery comparisons must account for measurement error, as different lotteries have different levels of precision for their MPLs.
Toward accounting for measurement error, I consider only dominance relations where the difference in expected value between the dominated and dominating lottery is at least as large as the maximum MPL measurement error between them: this yields, on average, 5.3 dominated lotteries for each(n1,n2)$(n_1, n_2)$comparison pair,n1,n2∈{2,4,8,16}$n_1, n_2 \in \lbrace 2,4,8,16\rbrace$. FigureA.3plots the frequency with which dominance is violated, on average, for each dominance relation, clustering standard errors by individual. The standard errors are too large to say much, though three directional results may be worth mentioning. First, consistent with simplicity theory, dominance is violated more often at 2 versus 16 outcomes than at 8 versus 16 outcomes. Second, and also consistent with the theory, in general there are fewer dominance violations when four-outcome lotteries are dominated than when 8 or 16-outcome lotteries are dominated. Third, and not captured by the support-based theory but allowed for by the general model of SectionIII.C, up to 1.25% of the time, dominance is violated for lotteries with the same support size.

Splitting Dominance Results by Cogn: FigureA.4splits participants by cognitive ability and plots within-subject dominance violations by complexity level, using the methodology of SectionI.E. There are two patterns in the data. First, participants with higher cognitive ability violate dominance less overall. Second, participants with lower cognitive ability display a pattern of more dominance violations as complexity increases; this pattern is less evident for those with higher cognitive ability, consistent with cognitive ability (or a correlate) possibly playing some role in affecting complexity aversion.

article.A.9. Skewness Preferences:
Moffatt, Sitzia, and Zizzo (2015) use lotteries from Sonsino, Benzion, and Mador (2002), both using the process of constructing lower variance, higher complexity lotteries and comparing these to higher variance, lower complexity lotteries. In particular, they claim that, within any comparison class containing a simple (S), complex (C), and very complex (VC) lottery, Var(S)>$>$Var(VC)>$>$Var(C). However, as I show below, within any of their comparison sets, Skew(S)>$>$Skew(VC)>$>$Skew(C) (see TableA.II). Thus, an agent with sufficiently strong skewness preferences would prefer the low complexity lottery in their data, not necessarily out of any complexity aversion, but because they like skewness.
Similarly, for the Huck and Weizsacker (1999) comparison pair of {2.29, 0.41; 1.39, 0.59} versus {2.21, 0.36; 1.51, 0.21; 1.11, 0.43}, the two-outcome lottery has both higher skewness and higher expected value.

Since the price lists have the same increments and range across number of outcomes, the actions that can be taken are similar regardless of complexity, and the only part of the model that remains that can vary with complexity isλ$\lambda$, the cost of information acquisition. article.A.13. Cognitive Noise: Efficient Coding:
I test whether efficient and adaptive coding can capture simplicity seeking via five tests, which speak to both the specific hypothesis of Frydman and Jin (2023) as a possible explanation for simplicity, as well as more generally to the idea that within-experiment experience effects may vary by complexity level.28
First, it may be the case that lotteries with different numbers of outcomes are associated with different priors (either in the natural component or fast-moving component), so that later risk taking is dependent on the number of 2-, 4-, 8-, or 16-outcome lotteries that the participant had earlier seen. To test this, following a split-and-test procedure in Frydman and Jin (2021, table III), for each individual, I partition lotteries in the risk module by whether the individual saw them in the first or the second half of the experiment.29I then test whether risk taking in the latter half depends on the number of lotteries at different complexity levels seen in the first half.
TableA.Vpresents the results. The outcome variable is the RRP ((EV − CE/EV) * 100); the observations are all lotteries each individual sees in the second half of the experiment; and the regressors are the lottery's own variance, skewness, and number of outcomes, as well as the number of lotteries at different complexity levels seen in the first half of the experiment.30The lottery's own properties (moments and number of outcomes) have statistically significant impacts on risk taking. Prior seen lotteries do not have much of an effect: the number of 2-, 8-, and 16-outcome lotteries seen in the first half of the experiment are not statistically significant. The number of four-outcome lotteries seen in the first half of the experiment has a statistically significant effect and reduces risk taking later in the experiment. Trying two other breakpoints (25% into the survey, and 75% into the survey, corresponding to columns 1 and 3 in TableA.V) for completeness yields quantitatively and statistically similar results for own properties, but no statistically significant result for the prior seen number of lotteries at any complexity level.
A second possibility is that both coding and retrieval differ by complexity level, so that individuals are partitioning lotteries by complexity classes when encoding (e.g., retrieving only the coding for two-outcome lotteries when faced with a two-outcome lottery). To test this, I regress the relative risk premia of each lottery at a given complexity level on its moments, number of outcomes, prior expected values seen at that complexity level, prior dispersion in expected values seen at that complexity level, and prior variance seen at that complexity level.
This test requires some care. First, in cases where the individual does not see any lotteries of a particular complexity level by the specified breakpoint, I drop that observation rather than imposing an (arbitrary) prior on expected value and variance; this does not have any effect for the specification 75%-in, and affects less than1%$1\%$of data for the 50%-in specification. For the 25%-in specification, this decision has a nontrivial effect because relatively few lotteries are seen by that point. Second, to avoid possible multicolinearity with number of outcomes (e.g., for individuals where the indicators used in interaction with characteristics with past lotteries can identify own number of outcomes—as can occur, if, for example, the prior expected values seen for two outcomes were 5 and 6, but the prior expected values seen for four outcomes were 7 and 7.5), I normalize each of the prior seen moments, so that, for example, prior seen average expected values(EVi,n)$s(\text{E}V_{i,n})$for individuali$i$at complexity leveln$n$becomess(EVi,n)−meanover individualsi′(s(EVi′,n)/stdover individualsi′(s(EVi′,n)$\left(s(\text{E}V_{i,n}) - \underset{\text{over individuals $i^{\prime }$}}{\text{mean}} (s(\text{E}V_{i^{\prime },n}) \right) / \underset{\text{over individuals $i^{\prime }$}}{\text{std}} (s(\text{E}V_{i^{\prime },n})$). This still allows for a test of the theory that higher or lower prior seen expected values (or other characteristics) affect later risk taking, and takes care of multicolinearity concerns. Third, in selecting which moments to use, I think of the agent as trying to figure out expected value,31for which (under the efficient coding hypothesis) the relevant statistics are the prior expected value and the prior dispersion in expected value. For robustness, at each breakpoint, I also try regressing risk taking on prior expected value and prior variance to get more generally at the idea that prior seen distributions at a particular complexity level affect later behavior at that complexity level.
TableA.VIshows the results. Here again, own moments and number of outcomes are statistically significant across specifications. The average expected value seen for prior two-outcome lotteries does not affect later two-outcome lotteries in a statistically significant way, nor for four-outcome lotteries. In both 75%-in specifications and one 25%-in specification, prior seen expected value for eight-outcome lotteries has a marginally statistically significant effect, with higher prior expected values increasing risk taking (e.g., decreasing RRP). Prior expected value for 16-outcome lotteries has a marginally statistically significant effect in the second 75%-in specification, but no others. Dispersion in prior expected value for eight-outcome lotteries has a marginally statistically significant effect at 25%-in, and prior variance for 16-outcome lotteries has a marginally statistically significant effect at 75%-in.
A third test is to check whether probability weighting in general differs by number of outcomes, as it should if participants have different priors by complexity level. AppendixA.10tests for this and finds that probability weights across number of outcomes are numerically similar and statistically indistinguishable.
A fourth possibility is that efficient coding is occurring, but in a manner that is not heavily dependent on complexity. To test this, TableA.VIIregresses RRP on moments, number of outcomes, and prior experienced expected value (across complexity levels) and dispersion in expected values (across complexity levels). As earlier, own number of outcomes is statistically significant across specifications. There is a marginally statistically significant loading on prior EV dispersion for the 25%-in specification, but own complexity remains a statistically significant determinant of risk premia across specifications.
A fifth test, for further robustness, considers rolling effects rather than a breakpoint. TableA.VIIIconsiders regressing RRP on the lottery's own characteristics, whether the most recent previously seen lottery had 2, 4, 8, or 16 outcomes, and the most recent previously seen lottery's moments. As in the first three tests, the lottery's own characteristics, including number of outcomes, has statistically significant effects. There is no statistically significant effect of the immediately prior lottery's characteristics.
These five tests cumulatively indicate that neither within-experiment experience effects nor complexity-dependent priors for probability weighting fully capture complexity aversion. One possible reason for the smaller dependence on prior experience here is that efficient coding predicts that coding is different when empirical distributions are different (a main experimental finding of Frydman and Jin (2021)). But by construction in this experiment, the moments of lotteries across complexity levels are similar. Nevertheless, there are hints of possible efficient coding that come up in some specifications, and it may be interesting to consider combinations of complexity and efficient coding in future work.

Many canonical decision theories model choice as deterministic, including PT, expected utility theory, and ambiguity aversion; the main text follows this approach. It is straightforward to extend simplicity to a stochastic representation, that is, one in which choices occur with noise. This section extends the cognitive tiers and generalized simplicity models of AppendicesCandA, respectively (which are themselves generalizations of simplicity) to stochastic representations. Stochastic variants of simplicity can generate additional interesting predictions, and I derive one example here. As earlier, I take the viewpoint that this is an as-if preference and make no claim on the specific neurological or other mechanisms that may further underlie simplicity-seeking behavior.
There are several ways to extend deterministic to stochastic models: just within expected utility, for example, one can think of the additive model (Gul and Pesendorfer (2006)), the logit model (Luce (1959)), or the dynamic history-dependent model (Chambers, Masatlioglu, and Turansick (2023)), among other variants. Here, we will formalize a stochastic simplicity Luce model, with the acknowledgment that there are also other ways to make simplicity stochastic, for example, the various methods of adding stochasticity cited above.
As in the previous section, letX$X$be an arbitrary space, andΔX$\Delta X$lotteries on this space. LetM(ΔX)$\mathcal {M}(\Delta X)$be the set of finite menus onΔX$\Delta X$; the analyst observes a choice frequencyρ$\rho$onM(ΔX)$\mathcal {M}(\Delta X)$, withρ(q,A)$\rho (q, A)$denoting the probability that the agent selects lotteryq$q$from menuA$A$.

Representations of this form admit straightforward axiomatizations, corresponding to the Luce axioms alongside axioms characterizingU$U$. As proof of concept, TheoremB.2provides a characterization of this stochastic variant for the case in whichU$U$is a generalized simplicity representation. Generally, the approach is to define a coarsening ofρ$\rho$, called⪰∗$\succeq ^*$, that corresponds to the majority selection in pairwise choice:p⪰∗q⇔ρp,{p,q}≥ρq,{p,q}$p \succeq ^* q \iff \rho \left(p, \lbrace p,q\rbrace \right) \ge \rho \left(q, \lbrace p,q\rbrace \right)$. By construction, if the analyst observesρ$\rho$, they can construct⪰∗$\succeq ^*$. As in the general simplicity representation, assume that lotteries are partitioned into cognitive tiers 1,2,…, and that any two lotteries with the same support have the same cognitive tier. Let≻$\succ$be a transitive, reflexive but possibly incomplete binary relation overΔX$\Delta X$, withX$X$an arbitrary underlying prize space.
The cognitive tiers representation says that, as complexity grows, the linear core of the preference reduces in size. Interpreting the linear core as the lotteries the DM is confident of their evaluation over, this means that the DM becomes less sure of their own judgment at higher levels of complexity. The representation does not impose any restrictions on completions, enabling it to capture as a special case many different ways of approaching complexity aversion. This flexibility may be useful as a building block for future work examining, either empirically or theoretically, alternative ways of approaching the subject.

Observe that this representation preserves continuity as long as tiers are continuous, for example, if ast1,t2,..→t$t_1, t_2,..\rightarrow t$,Ut1,Ut2→Ut$\mathcal {U}_{t_1}, \mathcal {U}_{t_2} \rightarrow \mathcal {U}_{t}$.

This special case encompasses as a further special case the cautious expected utility representation of Cerreia-Vioglio, Dillenberger, and Ortoleva (2015), but also allows the set of permissible utilities to grow as difficulty tier increases.


Example B.3. (Stochastic Within-Tier Effects)One could imagine that, for lotteries at which the preference is incomplete, the DM chooses uniformly at random. For example, within a high difficulty level, ifp$p$dominatesq$q$but the preference is incomplete over these, this stochastic framework would have the DM violate dominance within-tier at least some of the time.

As discussed above, these representations are more general than the additional constraints typically imposed by axiomatization; future work may wish to focus on some of the special cases described above.
